posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked:
I noticed I don’t have any interesting bottles to open with our son in a few years when he turns 21. Soliciting board suggestions regarding wines to seek.

Thanks!

posts.name: John Preston
posts.cooked:
I have a few to share with our son with the same birth year. Purchased several years ago…Togni and Dunn Howell Mountain Cabs. Also have a few bottles of Chapoutier Ermitage Le Pavillon. My plan was to uncork to celebrate various events in his life.

Did the same with our daughter with a 2001 birth year…she can look forward to Climens (Barsac), various German wines, and some of the same Calif. cabs.

Good luck!

Cheers,
JP

posts.name: David Culang
posts.cooked: Austria!

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:

I noticed I don’t have any interesting bottles to open with our son in a few years when he turns 21. Soliciting board suggestions regarding wines to seek.

Bruce

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
John Preston:

I have a few to share with our son with the same birth year. Purchased several years ago…Togni and Dunn Howell Mountain Cabs. Also have a few bottles of Chapoutier Ermitage Le Pavillon. My plan was to uncork to celebrate various events in his life.

Did the same with our daughter with a 2001 birth year…she can look forward to Climens (Barsac), various German wines, and some of the same Calif. cabs.

Good luck!

Cheers,
JP

Your daughter is lucky with a 2001 birth year; a fantastic vintage in Sauternes/Barsac in particular, and the Climens is an excellent call. If you can backfill on some well-stored 2001 La Tour Blanche, that’s a pretty good QPR version for 2001.

Bruce

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: 1997 Yquem is a great and under-rated wine.

posts.name: Loren Sonkin
posts.cooked: Italy was great in many regions. Some Barolos are very forward but the better ones are still babes. Gaja Sperss? Some very good if not great Ports as well. I agree with Jerry, the d’Yqeum is very good and can be found for a relatively good price.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Some regions to consider hunting through

97 Napa/Sonoma cabs
97 Sauternes
97 Tuscany

As others have mentioned, those categories don’t seem to have been bid up as high as more recent years.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Drank a 97 Egon Muller Spatlese tonight that had plenty still to give.

posts.name: Ed Steinway
posts.cooked:
Warren:

Our youngest child also has a 1997 birth year. We bought Montelena, Monte Bello and Dunn HM for California Cabs, Climens for Sauternes, and Taylor, Fonseca and Dow’s for Vintage Port. Have fun with the search!

Thanks,
Ed

posts.name: NED VALOIS
posts.cooked: Insignia & Backus , especially in Maggies !

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Agreed on Cal cabs for 1997. Great year. I’ve had a few really good '97 Barolos from Giacosa and G. Gonterno - these are drinking well now.

Another option is to look for higher end Bordeaux or Burgundy from 1997. I’ve had some great bottles and as this is considered a non-amazing year, deals can be had on some of the bigger names.

posts.name: Glenn L e v i n e
posts.cooked: I got a 97 MB upright now. Come down and you two can road test the bottle.
posts.display_username: Glenn L e v i n e

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked: Jadot did very well in 1997. the wines over perform.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: Port. The Noval 97 is fabulous.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: I like the way a lot of '97 Barolo have aged. The '97 red Burgs seem to be really coming into their own as well. Recent bottles of Arnoux RSV, de Vogue Chambolle 1er Cru and Faiveley Latricieres have all been top notch.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked: Agreed that 97 burgs have been impressive for me lately though I don’t know how long lived they’ll be. Most Bordeaux I’ve had has been mature, a but green and not usually all that great. I would go Cali. Just watch out, it was a ripe year and it was in the middle of Parker mania. 97 Shater HSS is a bit too ripe for me for example. Just double checked my notes and my highest rated 97 is Beringer PR. Excellent and readily available.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
Glenn L e v i n e:

I got a 97 MB upright now. Come down and you two can road test the bottle.

I had one a couple months ago Glenn and it was singing after some air.

Enjoy!

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: Chave!

posts.name: Peter Tryba
posts.cooked: Has anyone mentioned 1997 Noval yet?

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: It’s my anniversary year so I’ve been doing the same thing. Chave is the best thing I’ve tried so far for current drinking. Really good. Haven’t had a great red Burg from '97 yet (have tried several Jadot - Bonnes Mares and CSJ, DRC RSV, multiple Lafarge, holding out hope but haven’t had one yet. d’Yquem is relatively reasonably priced for a good year (sub-$300), just picked up one. CA sounds like a great idea but I haven’t started looking at that yet, normally not my thing.

posts.name: Fred Daniels
posts.cooked: Agreed it was a surprise year in Sauternes. Initially passed over, 97 (and increasingly 96) are showing as very good vintages indeed. The Yquem is a stunner…apparently they went through the vineyard like 15 times or more picking berry by berry. Others also successful like Climens, Rieussec and Raymond Lafon…and they are not crazy expensive although the Yquem has climbed in recent years. Please let us know if you find more winners.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
David Culang:

Austria!

Right!
However the best are now hard to find: Smaragds from the Wachau, some Sauvignon blancs from Styria (Sattlerhof Kranachberg is a miracle-wine, but even the producer is buying back single bottles cause he doesn´t have any left …
Tement Zieregg, Gross Nussberg … )
I have had some great from France: the very best might be
Pegau Cuvee Justine, Bonneau Marie Beurrier, Ogier Belle Helene, Chave, all Guigal Lalas, La Chapelle is fine, Chapoutier Pavillon, Jamet Cote brune and the regular CR … Rayas is wonderful now … however all are difficult to find now …

Many Burgundies are great to drink now, like RSV Arnoux (and Suchots) and Hudelot-Noellat as well as the Richebourg, Rousseau, Dujac Bonnes Mares and CdlRoche, CSDenis, Leroy … many others … however these wines are not for the eternity but should be drunk over the next 7, 10, maybe 12 years …
There are some fine Bordeaux, e.g. Pavie Macquin, Angelus, Evangile … more from right bank, but left bank are usually rather light …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked: Almost forgot about the Chave. An excellent classic Chave indeed.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Warren Taranow

I picked up a 1997 Comtes Lafon Volnay Santenots du Milieu and a '97 Taylor Fladgate on WineBid. Looking for others based on the great recommendations above.

posts.name: Jonathan H
posts.cooked: '97 Insignia I had recently was stunning!

posts.name: Jud Reis
posts.cooked:
NED VALOIS:

Insignia & Backus , especially in Maggies !

i would second Ned’s suggestion of Phelps Backus - it is an extraordinary bottle of wine that has many good years ahead of it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 1997 northern Rhones are drinking well. Jamet and Clape stand out in particular. Nice vintage to look at now.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked:
NED VALOIS:

Insignia & Backus , especially in Maggies !



Jud Reis:

i would second Ned’s suggestion of Phelps Backus - it is an extraordinary bottle of wine that has many good years ahead of it.

I just pulled the trigger on a magnum of Backus, thanks for the suggestion guys. Compared with Insignia, pricing is two-for-the-price-of-one, and in my limited experience quality is similar. Hope it’s not too over the top, it should make a good crowd-pleaser if we do a family celebration near our anniversary. I have a soft spot for Backus because my earliest revelation drinking a nicely mature red was the '78 in the late '80s at a restaurant I frequented. I remember going back and ordering that multiple times until they were gone .

Time to backfill some MB, and to start trying some good Barolo. The red Burgs I’ve tried from 1997 are a little coarse and dense, but have hope for the crown jewel of my small stash, a single bottle of Roumier Bonnes Mares. I didn’t buy more because it seemed expensive at 90 bucks .

(Edit-I agree with you Craig, still have a couple bottles of Chave red and one blanc!)
